articles.excerpt_nl: Hoe Meta's brillen, Flock-camera's en Ring-deurbellen bepalen wiens zichtbaarheid telt als een risico. Over een infrastructuur van bewaking die in stilte wordt opgebouwd, en over wie de prijs daarvan betaalt.
articles.excerpt_en: How Meta's glasses, Flock cameras and Ring doorbells decide whose visibility counts as a risk. On an infrastructure of surveillance built quietly into everyday life, and on who pays the price.
articles.body_nl:
*Deze essay is gepubliceerd in het Engels.* --- During my undergraduate studies, a professor brought in a projector and screened a short sci-fi film that would frighten me for years to come. The film was titled *Sight*. A man wakes up in a nearly empty apartment with bare walls, devoid of any decoration. It resembles a stage set, lacking the warmth and personal touch that really make a home feel like home. In the next scene, his augmented‑reality contact lenses flicker to life, and the room completely changes. Now his room is fully decorated. A constant stream of news and apps hovers at the edges of his vision. Recipes float above the chopping board as he cuts vegetables, and the act of cooking itself becomes a kind of game, with points and achievements blooming in his periphery. Suddenly, the entire atmosphere is filled with technology that can't be seen by the "naked" eye. The story unfolds as he gets ready for a date, with an increasing sense of dread dominating the space. Initially, the interface appears to be supportive, instructing him on what to cook, what to wear, and how to pass the time. When his date arrives, we come to understand that his lenses have been observing her as well. They pull up information about her that only he can see through his lenses. Prompts appear in his field of view that nudge him on what to do, how to posture himself, and what the best lines are for him to say. A first meeting, intended to be filled with uncertainty and spontaneity, transforms into a sequence of deliberate actions governed by an invisible structure. She is unaware of its existence, and even if she were, she could not perceive any of it. He, on the other hand, hardly seems to question or challenge it. By the end, the true terror is that he has allowed himself to become the individual the system has decided he should be to "achieve his desires".
I found myself restless for nights following that dystopian screening. Growing up, I considered surveillance as an external force linked to government structures, street cameras, and figures in uniforms. Not as something built into someone's eyes, secretly filming you. All this while shaping the encounter or relationship you share. In *Sight*, the device was an integral part of his existence rather than something that sat outside of him. I remember contemplating the possibility that this would actually happen. What if someone can walk into a room and scan every face they see for hidden data, unbeknownst to the other person? Fast forward to 2025, and that question emerges in a disturbingly mundane form: an internal memo. Meta's hardware division shared a document detailing an innovative feature for its smart glasses. They called it *[Name Tag](https://www.nytimes.com/2026/02/13/technology/meta-facial-recognition-smart-glasses.html)*. Through the glasses, a built‑in assistant scans the faces in front of you, searches Meta's databases, and returns a likely match, along with whatever information it can scrape together. Think of names, social media profiles, and public details tied to that identity. It is eerily familiar to the unsettling premise of *Sight*. But now the feature is in Ray-Ban frames and marketed as a way to "remember names" and "connect" with people around you. In the leaked memo, Meta acknowledges the 'safety and privacy risks' of the feature, then quickly shifts to the question of when to launch. Reportedly, after deliberating over the launch for almost a year, executives concluded that the "dynamic political environment" in the US, which includes deportation campaigns, culture-war upheaval, and depleted non-profits, presented an opportunity rather than a challenge. They did not want a calm moment in which civil‑society groups might have the bandwidth to focus on smart glasses. They wanted a moment when those groups were already stretched thin, fighting over immigration raids, abortion bans, and book removals from school libraries. Civil society groups were already pouring time and money into other fights, leaving little energy to contest a new pair of glasses. In that gap, facial recognition could slip discreetly into everyday life while most people were looking elsewhere. The idea was very simple but cold and calculated. While everyone is busy, slip facial recognition onto people's faces in places where politics stay turbulent, and the people who might resist are already worn down. More than 75 organisations, including the American Civil Liberties Union and the Electronic Privacy Information Centre, have since raised alarms about Meta's plans. They warn that incorporating facial recognition into consumer glasses would make it trivially easy to identify strangers at protests, outside clinics, near shelters, and at borders. In those spaces, anonymity is crucial for people seeking safety and protection. These glasses pose a serious risk and could endanger those people. This led US senators to demand answers. Meta's public response follows a well-known script. The company talks about "intentional" deployment, promises protective measures, and suggests launching first at a conference for people with visual impairments, as if accessibility could substitute for a more extensive discussion. Accessibility then becomes a kind of moral shield for a tool that, in different circumstances, makes public anonymity almost impossible. The memo shows that Meta knows exactly what it is doing. It chooses a moment when critics are overstretched and counting on exhaustion to soften the backlash. Meta relies on public fatigue to carry some of the weight of governance. If it were solely Meta making such a calculated decision, it might offer us some reassurance. However, the infrastructure that enabled *Name Tag* to exist was established well before Meta's glasses even existed. It has been assembled by companies that sold their products as safety tools, as well as by towns and households that accepted those tools in the name of 'security'. Flock Safety's automated licence-plate readers have been installed on roads and at the outer edges of parking lots throughout the United States. They are promoted as a means of recovering stolen vehicles and responding to "suspicious" behaviour. There was no national debate about whether small towns should join a larger, networked tracking system. Councils were informed that the cameras would be beneficial in the fight against crime, while residents were assured that this new system would provide a safer environment for their communities. The same networks that residents were told would catch car thieves are now in private hands and are accessible to local police and federal immigration agencies such as ICE and Border Patrol. They buy access to Flock's licence‑plate reader system, request footage from doorbell companies, and plug into data streams from telecoms and tech platforms. Much of this happens without public debate and, in many cases, without a warrant. It was only after journalists and researchers accessed query logs that the full extent of the system became visible. In Virginia, a recent investigation revealed that law enforcement conducted almost three thousand searches on Flock's network within one year. They did so using terms related to [immigration](https://www.404media.co/ice-taps-into-nationwide-ai-enabled-camera-network-data-shows/), such as "ICE," "ERO," and "deportee". A significant number of these searches were initiated at the request of federal agencies. People who thought they were paying for traffic safety discovered that their streets had become part of a statewide deportation net. In Washington state, [the University of Washington Center for Human Rights](https://jsis.washington.edu/humanrights/2025/10/21/leaving-the-door-wide-open/) uncovered at least eight local agencies that had discreetly enabled direct data‑sharing with the US Border Patrol. In a report titled *Leaving the Door Wide Open*, researchers describe this configuration as effectively opening a 'front door' for federal access. In theory, no one operates a mass‑surveillance programme. Flock can say it does not contract with ICE, while local police can say they are simply responding to lawful requests. Meanwhile, the Border Patrol can say it is using tools that communities chose. This kind of arrangement is hard to challenge because responsibility is spread out. On paper, no one is running a mass‑surveillance programme. In practice, the combined system makes ordinary streets feel more like checkpoints. Amazon's Ring uses the same kind of infrastructure but sells it with a softer, homier story. This year's Super Bowl ad showed a lost dog finding its way home with help from a Ring "Search Party" feature. In this ad, neighbourhood cameras stitch together a trail of clips until the dog appears at the right door. The story is designed to inspire a sense of belonging and togetherness, showing the neighbours' use of networked cameras to care for one another. Privacy advocates pointed out the troubling reality that a system designed to track a dog in a neighbourhood can just as easily be used to surveil individuals. Furthermore, concerns have been raised regarding Ring's planned partnership with Flock Safety, which would enable doorbell footage to be integrated into a network used for immigration enforcement. Following the commercial, the backlash was so strong that [Ring decided to terminate the partnership](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/02/12/amazons-ring-cancels-flock-partnership-amid-super-bowl-ad-backlash.html) before it even began. Both companies stressed that no customer footage had been shared. However, none of that changed the basic reality that countless cameras remained operational. Their footage is stored on corporate servers and can be searched at any time. Taken together, these systems show how technical infrastructure silently builds an archive of ordinary life. The next question is how shifts in law and politics decide what that archive can suddenly be used for.
When a company says it will keep data, it rarely means "for a short time". In practice, the footage does not go away. Imagine there is a march in 2024; back then, it draws little attention. Four years later, if a new administration comes into power, a law changes, or a particular group of people is newly defined as a problem. The same images can suddenly be revisited. Nobody has to decide in advance that this march is dangerous. It is enough that someone later types a term into a search bar and pulls up faces and licence plates from a moment when being there was legal. In 2022, the US Supreme Court's *Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization* decision overturned the federal protection for abortion. For almost fifty years, people had lived with abortion recognised as a constitutional right. Overnight, that baseline vanished, and individual states were free to ban the procedure entirely or allow it only under narrow conditions. Within months, roughly twenty states moved to ban or almost completely block abortion. In those states, abortion bans came either as total prohibitions or as six‑week limits that take effect before many people know they are pregnant. A procedure that was legal and available close to home in 2021 became a matter of crossing state lines. Money turns into a second barrier. You must find hundreds or even thousands of dollars for travel and time off work; otherwise, you carry an unwanted or dangerous pregnancy to term. In that sense, the law decides whose health, safety and family plans are still recognised by the state and whose are not. Even this single example is enough to see how changes in law can reshape people's lives, especially for marginalised groups. Suddenly, particular communities can be treated as a problem. The images from that same 2024 march can be used to identify these groups. When a march is organised for the LGBTQI community, or a protest in favour of abortion rights is captured by these surveillance devices, the images do not simply disappear. No one knows what will happen with that footage in the future. You do not need to have done anything wrong at the moment of recording to be treated as if you had later. It is enough that you were visible within the camera's range at a time and place that a platform chose to keep. This is especially evident for those who have learnt to regard visibility as a danger, rather than a protection. Research by groups such as [Privacy International](https://privacyinternational.org/long-read/5337/social-media-monitoring-uk-invisible-surveillance-tool-increasingly-deployed) shows how activists in the UK and across Europe change the way they move through the world. They take different routes to protests, turn down visible or leadership roles in movements, and some stop organising altogether because they know that cameras, facial‑recognition systems and social‑media monitoring can be used to build files on them. Researchers describe this as a "chilling effect". The phrase points to that moment when a person hesitates to act at all because they feel watched. The fear is not only arrest, although that fear is already serious. The deeper fear is the knowledge that somewhere a private company may hold a record that falls outside democratic control. No one can say with certainty what that company has kept or how those traces may be used one day. Ruha Benjamin's work makes it hard to pretend that this danger started with smartphones. It is not that history was peaceful, and then suddenly the devices changed that. That is to say, surveillance has always been a technology of control. Scholars in surveillance studies, particularly those working in critical race and infrastructure‑oriented traditions, have long argued that surveillance is not evenly distributed across bodies. They also show that new tools mostly refine older systems of control rather than replace them. *In Race After Technology*, she uses the phrase "New Jim Code" to describe technologies that present themselves as neutral or even progressive but, under the surface, treat white people as the default and consistently disadvantage people of colour. Instead of open segregation, this bias is now built into algorithms and scoring systems. These tools decide who gets housing, credit, policing or opportunities. The numbers on facial recognition make this hard to ignore. Independent audits of commercial systems have found that they make far more mistakes on Black women than on white men. In some evaluations, the difference in error rates between those groups is more than [thirty‑five percentage](https://mit-serc.pubpub.org/pub/bias-in-machine/release/1) points. A face the software reads as white and male tends to pass through as legible, easy to classify and unremarkable. A darker‑skinned woman is far more likely to be mismatched with someone else, flagged as uncertain, or pushed into an error category that marks her as a problem rather than a person. It can be tempting to describe that as a glitch, the kind of problem that better training data and a few more Black engineers could fix. However, Benjamin argues that these gaps are not accidents but part of a long pattern in which some people are kept fully visible to authorities, while others are allowed to remain partly hidden. Simone Browne shows what that pattern of surveillance looks like in the lives of Black people. In *Dark Matters*, she examines the tools that made slavery and colonial rule easier to control. She describes ship plans that mapped where enslaved people's bodies would be placed, marks burnt into skin to signal ownership, and newspaper ads that listed people's scars, heights and other details when they escaped. She also writes about lantern laws that forced Black New Yorkers to carry lanterns after dark so they were never fully out of sight. Together, these practices created a world in which some bodies were always exposed to the state, and others were not. When a police department now points a facial‑recognition camera at a crowd, it is continuing that way of looking rather than inventing a new one. In Benjamin and Browne's terms, facial recognition is another way of sorting people into those who must always be visible and those who can pass unnoticed. A system that keeps failing on certain faces is part of that order. It builds on Browne's line between people who are expected to be identifiable at all times and people who are allowed to move without constant checking. Some are treated as traceable wherever they go, so their presence in stations and on streets is always open to verification. Others move through the same spaces without each appearance being kept as a record. When the software produces a wrong match, the outcome is not something distant or theoretical. It can manifest as a stop that should never have happened, an arrest based on a faulty hit, a denied application, or even death. As we can see, the asymmetry manifests itself in more than just error rates. Additionally, the pattern determines who must bear the burden of permanent surveillance. Black organisers, Muslim communities, undocumented people, activists, sex workers, and queer and trans people in hostile political climates are treated as the main subjects of these systems. Surveillance has been tried out most heavily and consistently in their lives. When a town enters into a contract with Flock and then finds out that cameras have been searched thousands of times for immigration purposes, those searches do not fall evenly on everyone who uses those roads. The plates being checked belong, again and again, to the same kinds of drivers. When Ring footage is passed to police because someone has reported "suspicious" movement at a doorway, the face that ends up under scrutiny is more likely to be that of a courier, a neighbour, or a stranger already judged through a racialised sense of who is out of place on that street. Software in these systems does not independently decide whose life will be turned into data. The rules come from the world that produced the training sets. They reflect the streets where police have historically patrolled and the people whose complaints are treated as credible. Over time, they harden around certain neighbourhoods, the ones that are routinely described as dangerous. Once those patterns are built into a model, the system can repeat them at a speed and scale no human patrol could ever match. This is the context in which the line "nothing to hide, nothing to fear" falls apart. That reassurance depends on three beliefs at once: that the law will be applied evenly, that the definition of "wrong" will stay the same, and that the institutions doing the watching are harmless. Even a brief look at history shows how fragile those beliefs are. Laws move and change. A few examples are enough to see that. Trinidad and Tobago briefly decriminalised sex between men, only for a higher court to recriminalise it. The United States protected abortion at the federal level for almost fifty years and then removed that protection. From that point on, access to care was dictated by state lines and by money. What is legal one year can be condemned the next. Once archives exist, people can go back and read them against whatever is defined as "wrong" now. When I think about Name Tag, Flock and Ring, I keep coming back to the question of who gets to stay unseen. The question is not only what these systems see but who they allow to remain unseen. Some people still have the luxury of walking through a city and leaving no searchable trace. They can attend a march without their licence plate being stored in a database shared with federal agencies. When they go to a clinic, they do not have to fear that a neighbour's doorbell footage will later be used to reconstruct their route. If they sit in a bar, they do not have to worry that a stranger's glasses will scan their face. Others live with the opposite knowledge. Their visibility is already a liability, and every new camera on the street or on a neighbour's face becomes a place where their lives may be captured and stored. I mean something specific when I say that we have become part of the surveillance system. I do not mean that we woke up one morning as a secret police. The point is that the devices we wear and install help decide who never gets to slip out of sight. When you put on Meta's glasses in a crowded tram, you are not only exposing yourself to Meta's systems. You are also handing over the faces around you so they can be stored and searched later. When you accept Flock cameras on your street or share Ring clips in a neighbourhood app, you are not only contributing to "safety". You are helping to build an archive that will outlast this moment and this set of laws. The state can reach into that archive whenever it wants, and companies can keep drawing profit from it in the meantime. The fight over surveillance is no longer only about back‑end systems or written laws. Power now moves through habits, as cameras and networks fold into daily life and slowly change what people accept as normal. That shift does not fall evenly, because some lives already carry more attention from police, employers and neighbours than others. In that landscape, every new device that sharpens our view of those lives also deepens their exposure to institutions that have long treated their visibility as a signal to act, record, and punish. *If this resonates, I'd be curious to hear how you see it playing out where you live.* ## Sources Benjamin, Ruha. *Race After Technology: Abolitionist Tools for the New Jim Code.* Polity, 2019. Browne, Simone. *Dark Matters: On the Surveillance of Blackness.* Duke University Press, 2015. "Meta Plans to Add Facial Recognition Technology to Its Smart Glasses." *The New York Times,* 13 Feb. 2026, . "ICE Taps into Nationwide AI-Enabled Camera Network, Data Shows." *404 Media,* 26 May 2025, . "Leaving the Door Wide Open: Flock Surveillance Systems Expose Local Communities to Federal Immigration Enforcement." *University of Washington Center for Human Rights,* 20 Oct. 2025, . "Amazon's Ring Cancels Flock Partnership amid Super Bowl Ad Backlash." *CNBC,* 12 Feb. 2026, . "The Bias in the Machine: Facial Recognition Technology and Racial Disparities." *MIT Schwarzman College of Computing / SERC,* 4 Feb. 2021, . "Social Media Monitoring in the UK: The Invisible Surveillance Tool Increasingly Deployed." *Privacy International,* 16 July 2024, . --- *This essay is also published on [Substack](https://dawin.substack.com/p/who-gets-to-stay-unseen).*

In some evaluations, the difference in error rates between those groups is more than [thirty‑five percentage](https://mit-serc.pubpub.org/pub/bias-in-machine/release/1) points. A face the software reads as white and male tends to pass through as legible, easy to classify and unremarkable. A darker‑skinned woman is far more likely to be mismatched with someone else, flagged as uncertain, or pushed into an error category that marks her as a problem rather than a person. It can be tempting to describe that as a glitch, the kind of problem that better training data and a few more Black engineers could fix. However, Benjamin argues that these gaps are not accidents but part of a long pattern in which some people are kept fully visible to authorities, while others are allowed to remain partly hidden. Simone Browne shows what that pattern of surveillance looks like in the lives of Black people. In *Dark Matters*, she examines the tools that made slavery and colonial rule easier to control. She describes ship plans that mapped where enslaved people's bodies would be placed, marks burnt into skin to signal ownership, and newspaper ads that listed people's scars, heights and other details when they escaped. She also writes about lantern laws that forced Black New Yorkers to carry lanterns after dark so they were never fully out of sight. Together, these practices created a world in which some bodies were always exposed to the state, and others were not. When a police department now points a facial‑recognition camera at a crowd, it is continuing that way of looking rather than inventing a new one. In Benjamin and Browne's terms, facial recognition is another way of sorting people into those who must always be visible and those who can pass unnoticed. A system that keeps failing on certain faces is part of that order. It builds on Browne's line between people who are expected to be identifiable at all times and people who are allowed to move without constant checking. Some are treated as traceable wherever they go, so their presence in stations and on streets is always open to verification. Others move through the same spaces without each appearance being kept as a record. When the software produces a wrong match, the outcome is not something distant or theoretical. It can manifest as a stop that should never have happened, an arrest based on a faulty hit, a denied application, or even death. As we can see, the asymmetry manifests itself in more than just error rates. Additionally, the pattern determines who must bear the burden of permanent surveillance. Black organisers, Muslim communities, undocumented people, activists, sex workers, and queer and trans people in hostile political climates are treated as the main subjects of these systems. Surveillance has been tried out most heavily and consistently in their lives. When a town enters into a contract with Flock and then finds out that cameras have been searched thousands of times for immigration purposes, those searches do not fall evenly on everyone who uses those roads. The plates being checked belong, again and again, to the same kinds of drivers. When Ring footage is passed to police because someone has reported "suspicious" movement at a doorway, the face that ends up under scrutiny is more likely to be that of a courier, a neighbour, or a stranger already judged through a racialised sense of who is out of place on that street. Software in these systems does not independently decide whose life will be turned into data. The rules come from the world that produced the training sets. They reflect the streets where police have historically patrolled and the people whose complaints are treated as credible. Over time, they harden around certain neighbourhoods, the ones that are routinely described as dangerous. Once those patterns are built into a model, the system can repeat them at a speed and scale no human patrol could ever match. This is the context in which the line "nothing to hide, nothing to fear" falls apart. That reassurance depends on three beliefs at once: that the law will be applied evenly, that the definition of "wrong" will stay the same, and that the institutions doing the watching are harmless. Even a brief look at history shows how fragile those beliefs are. Laws move and change. A few examples are enough to see that. Trinidad and Tobago briefly decriminalised sex between men, only for a higher court to recriminalise it. The United States protected abortion at the federal level for almost fifty years and then removed that protection. From that point on, access to care was dictated by state lines and by money. What is legal one year can be condemned the next. Once archives exist, people can go back and read them against whatever is defined as "wrong" now. When I think about Name Tag, Flock and Ring, I keep coming back to the question of who gets to stay unseen. The question is not only what these systems see but who they allow to remain unseen. Some people still have the luxury of walking through a city and leaving no searchable trace. They can attend a march without their licence plate being stored in a database shared with federal agencies. When they go to a clinic, they do not have to fear that a neighbour's doorbell footage will later be used to reconstruct their route. If they sit in a bar, they do not have to worry that a stranger's glasses will scan their face. Others live with the opposite knowledge. Their visibility is already a liability, and every new camera on the street or on a neighbour's face becomes a place where their lives may be captured and stored. I mean something specific when I say that we have become part of the surveillance system. I do not mean that we woke up one morning as a secret police. The point is that the devices we wear and install help decide who never gets to slip out of sight. When you put on Meta's glasses in a crowded tram, you are not only exposing yourself to Meta's systems. You are also handing over the faces around you so they can be stored and searched later. When you accept Flock cameras on your street or share Ring clips in a neighbourhood app, you are not only contributing to "safety". You are helping to build an archive that will outlast this moment and this set of laws. The state can reach into that archive whenever it wants, and companies can keep drawing profit from it in the meantime. The fight over surveillance is no longer only about back‑end systems or written laws. Power now moves through habits, as cameras and networks fold into daily life and slowly change what people accept as normal. That shift does not fall evenly, because some lives already carry more attention from police, employers and neighbours than others. In that landscape, every new device that sharpens our view of those lives also deepens their exposure to institutions that have long treated their visibility as a signal to act, record, and punish. *If this resonates, I'd be curious to hear how you see it playing out where you live.* ## Sources Benjamin, Ruha. *Race After Technology: Abolitionist Tools for the New Jim Code.* Polity, 2019. Browne, Simone. *Dark Matters: On the Surveillance of Blackness.* Duke University Press, 2015. "Meta Plans to Add Facial Recognition Technology to Its Smart Glasses." *The New York Times,* 13 Feb. 2026, . "ICE Taps into Nationwide AI-Enabled Camera Network, Data Shows." *404 Media,* 26 May 2025, . "Leaving the Door Wide Open: Flock Surveillance Systems Expose Local Communities to Federal Immigration Enforcement." *University of Washington Center for Human Rights,* 20 Oct. 2025, . "Amazon's Ring Cancels Flock Partnership amid Super Bowl Ad Backlash." *CNBC,* 12 Feb. 2026, . "The Bias in the Machine: Facial Recognition Technology and Racial Disparities." *MIT Schwarzman College of Computing / SERC,* 4 Feb. 2021, . "Social Media Monitoring in the UK: The Invisible Surveillance Tool Increasingly Deployed." *Privacy International,* 16 July 2024, . --- *This essay is also published on [Substack](https://dawin.substack.com/p/who-gets-to-stay-unseen).*

articles.title_nl: Reloading: dekoloniale narratieven binnen de blockchain
articles.title_en: Reloading: decolonial narratives within the blockchain
articles.excerpt_nl: Een onderzoek naar hoe de NFT-tentoonstelling Reloading zich verhoudt tot dekoloniale narratieven binnen blockchain-technologie. Ontmantelt of reproduceert deze technologie bestaande machtsstructuren?
articles.excerpt_en: Research into how the NFT exhibition Reloading engages with decolonial narratives within blockchain technology. Does this technology dismantle or reproduce existing power structures?
articles.body_nl:
*Een uittreksel uit mijn masterthesis aan de Universiteit van Amsterdam, New Media & Digital Culture, 2022.*

Sinds de doorbraak van non-fungible tokens — kortweg NFT's — in 2021 is er een verhaal in omloop dat klinkt als een belofte. De technologie zou digitale kunstenaars eindelijk de mogelijkheid geven om hun werk te verkopen zonder tussenkomst van galeries, veilinghuizen of de traditionele poortwachters van de kunstwereld.

Het klinkt utopisch. En precies daarom is het de moeite waard om er een stap dichterbij te kijken.

## De onderzoeksvraag

In mijn thesis stel ik de vraag: hoe verhoudt het NFT-project *Reloading* — een samenwerking tussen ART X Lagos en het NFT-platform SuperRare — zich tot dekoloniale narratieven die gekleurd worden door blockchain-imaginaries? Anders gezegd: wat doen Afrikaanse kunstenaars met deze technologie, en wat doet de technologie met hen?

## Wat blockchain wel en niet doet

Een van de meest taaie misverstanden over blockchain is dat het inherent decentraal zou zijn. Dat is afhankelijk van het soort blockchain. *Permissionless* blockchains zijn open voor iedereen. *Permissioned* blockchains hebben poortwachters, en het zijn juist die laatste die door overheden en grote bedrijven worden omarmd.

## Wat blijft hangen

Wat ik aan deze thesis heb overgehouden is geen conclusie maar een methode. De vraag is niet *"is deze technologie goed of slecht?"*. De vraag is: welke machtsverhoudingen lopen er op dit moment door deze technologie heen, wie heeft daar baat bij, en wie wordt erin gevangen?

---

[Lees de volledige thesis (PDF)](dania-awin-thesis-2022.pdf)
articles.body_en:
*An excerpt from my master's thesis at the University of Amsterdam, New Media & Digital Culture, 2022.*

Since the breakthrough of non-fungible tokens — NFTs for short — in 2021, a story has circulated that sounds like a promise. The technology would finally allow digital artists to sell their work without intermediaries — without galleries, auction houses, or the traditional gatekeepers of the art world.

It sounds utopian. Which is exactly why it's worth looking closer.

## The research question

In my thesis I ask: how does the NFT project *Reloading* — a collaboration between ART X Lagos and the NFT marketplace SuperRare — engage with decolonial narratives coloured by blockchain imaginaries? In other words: what are African artists doing with this technology, and what is the technology doing to them?

## What blockchain does and doesn't do

One of the stickiest misunderstandings about blockchain is that it is inherently decentralised. That depends on the kind of blockchain. *Permissionless* blockchains are open to everyone. *Permissioned* blockchains have gatekeepers — and it is precisely the latter that governments and large corporations have embraced.

## What stays with me

What I took from this thesis is not a conclusion but a method. The question is not *"is this technology good or bad?"*. The question is: which power relations are currently running through this technology, who benefits, and who is caught in it?

---

[Read the full thesis (PDF)](dania-awin-thesis-2022.pdf)

articles.title_nl: Wanneer inclusie taal wordt en stopt bij macht
articles.title_en: When inclusion becomes language and stops at power
articles.excerpt_nl: Over instituties die de taal van diversiteit overnemen zonder hun hiërarchieën los te laten, en waarom DEI zo vaak blijft hangen in woorden.
articles.excerpt_en: On institutions that speak diversity while keeping their hierarchies, and why DEI so often becomes language without redistribution of power.
articles.body_nl:
*Deze essay is gepubliceerd in het Engels. Schakel rechtsboven naar EN voor de volledige tekst, of lees hieronder.*

---

*This essay is part of an ongoing series of living research. It exists both on paper and in practice. I invite your reflections, questions, and disagreements, and hope it can be a space where dialogue continues beyond the page.*

I did not begin this work inside institutions. As a student, I was part of a grassroots initiative that set out to decolonise the curriculum. We worked with universities, museums, and other organisations, but I was wary of being on the inside. I did not believe that change could come from within the very structures that produced the problem. Years later, when I joined a large, self-described progressive organisation, I saw how familiar that logic was: colleagues spoke passionately about justice and equality, but the organisation remained centred on white, highly educated norms. The gap between its public commitments and its internal life was impossible to ignore.

In many large, self-described progressive organisations — from NGOs to universities and cultural institutions — diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) have become part of the organisational moral self-image. Its importance is woven into the organisation's public life, from the way it describes itself in official documents to the way it presents itself in recruitment and external communication. On the surface, there is often broad agreement that inclusion, representation, and equity matter. What is striking, however, is how rarely this rhetorical agreement translates into the kinds of structural changes that DEI, in its most serious sense, actually demands. Because the question is never only about who is present, but whose voices carry authority, how power is distributed, and what forms of experience and knowledge are allowed to count as expertise. In Patricia Hill Collins's terms, the struggle over inclusion is also a struggle over epistemic authority: whose standpoint is treated as a legitimate source of knowledge, and whose insights remain marginal or conditional (Collins, 2019). The contradiction reveals itself clearly when inclusion shifts from being a mere statement of intent to a required action that organisations must undertake. Embracing diversity as a principle is a relatively easy endeavour. Inclusion transforms into a complex challenge when it transcends rhetorical affirmation in words, demanding instead an actual institutional change. This rarely encounters outright rejection. What unfolds, however, is more straightforward. It is uncommon for individuals to express a direct refusal. Across different organisations, the responses are remarkably consistent. Inclusion remains a concept that is celebrated in theory, yet its actualisation is perpetually deferred.

There is a particular kind of meeting in which this dynamic plays out almost predictably. Here, the community actively participates in ethical conversation, and everyone acknowledges the importance of equity and fairness. But the moment inclusion begins to imply the redistribution of power, the room begins to tighten up. It is not that anyone explicitly says no. Instead, they often show their reluctance by being hesitant rather than outright refusing. After that, you'll notice that the discussion slows down and becomes more careful. In one organisation where I worked, this took the form of repeated objections whenever racial justice and leadership were raised. Proposals to centre race in strategy were met with worries about "losing our current base" or "alienating supporters," and suggestions to appoint more leaders of colour were blocked with familiar phrases: "we can't find the right candidates," "we need someone now." An external DEI bureau was eventually brought in, but their work focused on gossip and interpersonal tensions rather than the structural conditions that made people of colour feel unheard. The discomfort of white colleagues became the main object of concern, while the experiences of people of colour were further sidelined. What began as a subject of structural change evolves into a question of tone, procedure, and how to conduct the conversation appropriately. In these situations, inclusion is still validated, but only if it does not disrupt what has already been established. The institution can accommodate diversity as a desire more easily than as a requirement. The focus shifts from what needs to change to how change might be communicated without unsettling those who are most accustomed to being comfortable. At that point, the limits of the institution's commitment become visible.

As Sara Ahmed reminds us, this resistance is not always loud or identifiable, but it is structural, sedimented in what she calls the brick wall of institutional life. Diversity practitioners often describe their work as "banging your head against a brick wall," a phrase Ahmed uses to theorise how institutions exert their own kind of passivity. The wall is not necessarily built out of open hostility but out of habit, repetition, and what she calls the "background ease" of institutional life. This is the sense that things already make sense as they are. Diversity work, then, becomes work against the grain of institutional automatisms, work that makes visible what the institution has naturalized as "just how we do things here." The more persistently practitioners push for change, the more tangible the wall becomes; the institution reveals its friction precisely when challenged to act differently (Ahmed, 2012, pp. 23–27). This is exactly the institutional mechanism Ahmed theorises in *On Being Included* (2012).

Ahmed describes diversity commitments as "non-performatives": meaning statements that express a desire to act ethically but fail to translate that intention into action (Ahmed, 2012, pp. 16–18). Rather than serving as a catalyst for changing an institution's actions, commitment transforms into a fundamental aspect of the institution's self-description. So, you see, DEI is not absent but absorbed. Instead of being a force that compels the institution to change, it becomes proof that the institution is good. Ahmed makes more than just the claim that institutions are dishonest. It is much more unsettling than that. The institution can take up the terminology of critique without actually allowing critique to do its work. DEI becomes institutional language, even while practitioners continue to experience the institution as resistant to their labour (Ahmed, 2012, pp. 16–18). As Ahmed puts it, the paradox is that diversity becomes omnipresent as an official language at the very moment practitioners encounter the institution as immovable. The very fact that organisations hire diversity officers, set up committees, and produce policy documents can become a sign of this paradox. Practitioners are told to "make diversity a goal", which is already an indication that diversity is not yet an institutional given. The hiring of a diversity officer may even signal the absence of broader support, as if the organisation can claim legitimacy by simply having someone "just there" (Ahmed, 2012, p. 22). This helps explain why DEI so often becomes concentrated in the domain of communication and symbolism. Not necessarily because individuals inside organisations are cynical, but because institutions govern themselves through risk, reputation, and continuity. NGOs, in particular, rely on moral legitimacy. Their survival depends on donors, partnerships, stakeholder confidence, and public trust. In that context, it makes institutional sense to speak the language of inclusion. Silence is costly, and so is speaking incorrectly. But adopting the right language is comparatively inexpensive, so long as it does not become a binding demand for the redistribution of power. Here, an important contrast between inclusion as performance and inclusion as governance becomes apparent. Performance is about how an organisation presents itself and how it gets an ethical image. While governance relates to the structure of power: who decides, who takes charge, how resources are distributed, what constitutes professionalism, how institutional norms are perpetuated, and which accountability systems are really important. A lot of institutions are prepared to spend money on performance. Far fewer are willing to pursue governance-level transformation because governance change always involves the issue of who must give up comfort, power, or prestige.

The frequent appeal to the "business case" for diversity often highlights this contradiction. In institutional discourse, the business case refers to the idea that inclusion is not only morally desirable but also has economic advantages. DEI is justified in terms of performance, innovation, profitability, and organisational resilience rather than justice alone. In the last ten years, the discourse surrounding consultancy reporting and management research has consistently drawn upon this argument. McKinsey's influential reports, *Diversity Matters* (2015), *Delivering Through Diversity* (2018), and *Diversity Wins* (2020), draw on large datasets of more than 1,000 companies across different regions and sectors, and they identify a clear correlation between leadership diversity and the likelihood of financial outperformance. While it is important to acknowledge that these results are correlational and not strictly causal, they have increasingly been treated as central to the dominant narrative that inclusion is beneficial both ethically and practically (McKinsey & Company, 2015, 2018, 2020). At the same time, critical scholars have warned that the "business case" can be fragile. Mike Noon, for example, argues that pinning equality to commercial returns risks undermining it: if the numbers no longer support a performance gain, the moral claim to justice becomes easier to discard (Noon, 2007, pp. 773–775).

In this instance, it is crucial to recognise that the presence of evidence does not cancel out the existence of resistance. Considering that diversity holds both moral significance and practical benefits, what accounts for the ongoing resistance within institutions? In most cases, a lack of data is not the answer. The answer always lies in power. Organisational strength may be correlated with diversity, but forms of dominance that are not easily surrendered are threatened by the redistribution of that power. While an institution may reap advantages as a whole, certain groups may see inclusion as a threat to their unwritten standards, such as the status quo, the influence of familiarity with the culture, or the ease with which one may define professionalism according to one's own image. For this reason, DEI initiatives so often collapse into training programmes: workshops, listening sessions, mandatory modules. Training is not meaningless, but it is institutionally appealing because it relocates the problem to the realm of interpersonal sensitivity and individual attitudes. It creates the appearance of movement without requiring structural confrontation. Organisational research has long been sceptical of such approaches when they are not paired with accountability mechanisms. Frank Dobbin and Alexandra Kalev, for example, draw on three decades of data from more than 800 U.S. firms and hundreds of interviews with managers to show that many standard diversity programmes fail precisely because they do not change the systems through which organisations hire, promote, and assign responsibility, and that mandatory training can even generate backlash rather than durable change (Dobbin & Kalev, 2016, pp. 53–58). One of the clearest expressions of institutional resistance is the persistent claim that "there are not enough qualified candidates" from underrepresented groups. Scarcity is rarely the real issue. The real problem is recognition. Leadership is reproduced through cultural codes that appear neutral but are not, through informal expectations about what authority sounds like, where competence is assumed to reside, and who is trusted without explanation. Diversity becomes acceptable so long as it does not disrupt these norms. Difference may be welcomed, but transformation is not.

This institutional pattern is not confined to Anglo-American contexts. Similar dynamics, shaped by local histories and norms, appear in Dutch organisational life as well. In *Leiderschap in Kleur*, Judi Mesman draws on qualitative interviews with forty leaders of colour in the Dutch (semi-)public sector to describe what she calls "white standards and expectations": the persistent pressure to adapt to prevailing institutional norms in environments that offer little space for difference on its own terms (Mesman, 2022, pp. 2–3). Participants describe a double burden. They are expected to lead, while simultaneously having to navigate otherness, misunderstanding, and a lack of support from above, becoming in Nirmal Puwar's terms "space invaders": bodies that enter spaces historically reserved for white, male norms, whose very presence exposes how those spaces were never neutral to begin with (Mesman, 2022, p. 3; Puwar, 2004). Mesman also points to how organisations can use a small number of leaders of colour to suggest that the problem has been solved, even when underlying norms and structures remain largely unchanged, turning representation into a form of tokenism rather than shared responsibility for change. Mesman's conclusion is blunt in its simplicity: diversity and inclusion are not achieved by placing a single person of colour in a leadership position. Without mandate, shared responsibility, and structural change in organisational culture, representation becomes another form of isolation (Mesman, 2022, p. 4).

What emerges, then, is not primarily a story of individual malice, but a structural one. Institutions can present themselves as inclusive while remaining organised around inherited hierarchies. Diversity becomes less a redistribution of power than a form of institutional virtue, something the organisation can point to as evidence of goodness. As Audre Lorde puts it, "the master's tools will never dismantle the master's house": practices that leave the underlying structures intact may offer recognition or comfort, but they do not alter the terms on which power is organised (Lorde, 1984, p. 112). The organisation remains largely the same, even as the vocabulary of inclusion expands around it. Inclusion is welcomed, but only as long as it does not require the institution to change how power actually works.

---

## References

Ahmed, S. (2012). *On Being Included: Racism and Diversity in Institutional Life.* Durham, NC: Duke University Press.

Collins, P. H. (2019). *Intersectionality as Critical Social Theory.* Durham, NC: Duke University Press.

Dobbin, F., & Kalev, A. (2016). Why diversity programs fail. *Harvard Business Review,* 94(7–8), 52–60.

Lorde, A. (1984). The master's tools will never dismantle the master's house. In *Sister Outsider: Essays and Speeches* (pp. 110–114). Freedom, CA: Crossing Press.

McKinsey & Company. (2015). *Diversity Matters.* McKinsey & Company.

McKinsey & Company. (2018). *Delivering Through Diversity.* McKinsey & Company.

McKinsey & Company. (2020). *Diversity Wins: How Inclusion Matters.* McKinsey & Company.

Mesman, J. (2024). *Leiderschap in kleur: Ervaringen van mensen van kleur in leiderschapsposities in de (semi-)publieke sector.* Leiden: Universiteit Leiden / Algemene Bestuursdienst.

Noon, M. (2007). The fatal flaws of diversity and the business case for ethnic minorities. *Work, Employment and Society,* 21(4), 773–784.

Puwar, N. (2004). *Space Invaders: Race, Gender and Bodies Out of Place.* Oxford: Berg.

---

*This essay is also published on [Substack](https://substack.com/@daniaawin/p-193574268).*

In the last ten years, the discourse surrounding consultancy reporting and management research has consistently drawn upon this argument. McKinsey's influential reports, *Diversity Matters* (2015), *Delivering Through Diversity* (2018), and *Diversity Wins* (2020), draw on large datasets of more than 1,000 companies across different regions and sectors, and they identify a clear correlation between leadership diversity and the likelihood of financial outperformance. While it is important to acknowledge that these results are correlational and not strictly causal, they have increasingly been treated as central to the dominant narrative that inclusion is beneficial both ethically and practically (McKinsey & Company, 2015, 2018, 2020). At the same time, critical scholars have warned that the "business case" can be fragile. Mike Noon, for example, argues that pinning equality to commercial returns risks undermining it: if the numbers no longer support a performance gain, the moral claim to justice becomes easier to discard (Noon, 2007, pp. 773–775).

In this instance, it is crucial to recognise that the presence of evidence does not cancel out the existence of resistance. Considering that diversity holds both moral significance and practical benefits, what accounts for the ongoing resistance within institutions? In most cases, a lack of data is not the answer. The answer always lies in power. Organisational strength may be correlated with diversity, but forms of dominance that are not easily surrendered are threatened by the redistribution of that power. While an institution may reap advantages as a whole, certain groups may see inclusion as a threat to their unwritten standards, such as the status quo, the influence of familiarity with the culture, or the ease with which one may define professionalism according to one's own image. For this reason, DEI initiatives so often collapse into training programmes: workshops, listening sessions, mandatory modules. Training is not meaningless, but it is institutionally appealing because it relocates the problem to the realm of interpersonal sensitivity and individual attitudes. It creates the appearance of movement without requiring structural confrontation. Organisational research has long been sceptical of such approaches when they are not paired with accountability mechanisms. Frank Dobbin and Alexandra Kalev, for example, draw on three decades of data from more than 800 U.S. firms and hundreds of interviews with managers to show that many standard diversity programmes fail precisely because they do not change the systems through which organisations hire, promote, and assign responsibility, and that mandatory training can even generate backlash rather than durable change (Dobbin & Kalev, 2016, pp. 53–58). One of the clearest expressions of institutional resistance is the persistent claim that "there are not enough qualified candidates" from underrepresented groups. Scarcity is rarely the real issue. The real problem is recognition. Leadership is reproduced through cultural codes that appear neutral but are not, through informal expectations about what authority sounds like, where competence is assumed to reside, and who is trusted without explanation. Diversity becomes acceptable so long as it does not disrupt these norms. Difference may be welcomed, but transformation is not.

This institutional pattern is not confined to Anglo-American contexts. Similar dynamics, shaped by local histories and norms, appear in Dutch organisational life as well. In *Leiderschap in Kleur*, Judi Mesman draws on qualitative interviews with forty leaders of colour in the Dutch (semi-)public sector to describe what she calls "white standards and expectations": the persistent pressure to adapt to prevailing institutional norms in environments that offer little space for difference on its own terms (Mesman, 2022, pp. 2–3). Participants describe a double burden. They are expected to lead, while simultaneously having to navigate otherness, misunderstanding, and a lack of support from above, becoming in Nirmal Puwar's terms "space invaders": bodies that enter spaces historically reserved for white, male norms, whose very presence exposes how those spaces were never neutral to begin with (Mesman, 2022, p. 3; Puwar, 2004). Mesman also points to how organisations can use a small number of leaders of colour to suggest that the problem has been solved, even when underlying norms and structures remain largely unchanged, turning representation into a form of tokenism rather than shared responsibility for change. Mesman's conclusion is blunt in its simplicity: diversity and inclusion are not achieved by placing a single person of colour in a leadership position. Without mandate, shared responsibility, and structural change in organisational culture, representation becomes another form of isolation (Mesman, 2022, p. 4).

What emerges, then, is not primarily a story of individual malice, but a structural one. Institutions can present themselves as inclusive while remaining organised around inherited hierarchies. Diversity becomes less a redistribution of power than a form of institutional virtue, something the organisation can point to as evidence of goodness. As Audre Lorde puts it, "the master's tools will never dismantle the master's house": practices that leave the underlying structures intact may offer recognition or comfort, but they do not alter the terms on which power is organised (Lorde, 1984, p. 112). The organisation remains largely the same, even as the vocabulary of inclusion expands around it. Inclusion is welcomed, but only as long as it does not require the institution to change how power actually works.

---

Lorde, A. (1984). The master's tools will never dismantle the master's house. In *Sister Outsider: Essays and Speeches* (pp. 110–114). Freedom, CA: Crossing Press.

McKinsey & Company. (2015). *Diversity Matters.* McKinsey & Company.

McKinsey & Company. (2018). *Delivering Through Diversity.* McKinsey & Company.

McKinsey & Company. (2020). *Diversity Wins: How Inclusion Matters.* McKinsey & Company.

Mesman, J. (2024). *Leiderschap in kleur: Ervaringen van mensen van kleur in leiderschapsposities in de (semi-)publieke sector.* Leiden: Universiteit Leiden / Algemene Bestuursdienst.

Puwar, N. (2004). *Space Invaders: Race, Gender and Bodies Out of Place.* Oxford: Berg.

articles.title_nl: Verantwoordelijkheid als een daad van liefde
articles.title_en: Accountability as an Act of Love
articles.excerpt_nl: Over het verschil tussen straf en verantwoordelijkheid, en waarom iemand met liefde aanspreken een van de tederste vormen van zorg kan zijn.
articles.excerpt_en: On the difference between punishment and accountability, and why holding someone to account with love can be one of the most tender forms of regard.
articles.body_nl:
*Deze essay is gepubliceerd in het Engels.*

---

This essay began, as many things in my life do, with a conversation with my best friend while I sat in a café. Neither of us have children, but we found ourselves talking about how we would want to raise them: what it might mean to teach a child that mistakes are survivable and that being held to account does not have to mean being cast out. As we talked, I thought about the times we had lost and found each other again and again after conflict.

Relationships have never been a neutral topic for me. I grew up in a house where love did not arrive alone. Danger sat at the same table, punishment was ordinary, and a child learnt early that safety could vanish without warning. In that world, being wrong did not mean "I made a mistake." It meant "I might not survive this."

For years, that training came with me into every relationship I touched. Before anyone else could punish me, I did it myself, convinced that if something went wrong it must be my fault, that something in me was rotten. In the same breath, I punished others when they hurt me. Someone who disappointed me became a villain in my mind. When conflict appeared, I disappeared. I cut contact and found it almost impossible to stay open. No one had ever shown me how to do that.

Healing did not begin as a noble wish to become a better person. It began as a survival project, a question of how to stay alive in myself and how to function at all. Only later did that work grow into a different kind of ambition. I wanted to live in a world, to build a life and a set of relationships, that were as far as possible from the patterns I inherited. Slowly, I have been relearning how to stay in a friendship after conflict, how to remain in contact and find a way back to each other when things are hard. In the place of my deepest wound, my family, I am still learning what that could mean.

Out of that history, accountability has never felt like a safe word. I have seen what punishment does to a child, and what it leaves behind in the adult that child becomes. I have also seen how quickly righteous anger turns other people into enemies, how easily pain becomes a reason to cut someone off instead of a reason to speak. One question keeps returning. How do we approach accountability in a way that does not repeat the violence I escaped, and at the same time does not pretend that harm is harmless?

My story is only one version of this, yet it rests inside a larger pattern. Many of us brace ourselves as soon as accountability enters the room. The words arrive dressed as punishment. What begins as "you did something harmful" is heard as "you are harmful," and "this behaviour must change" turns into "your character is on trial." Under those conditions, even necessary confrontation can feel like annihilation. Therefore, defensiveness, denial, and self-justification are not mysterious responses. They belong to people who have been taught, again and again, that wrongdoing makes them unworthy of belonging.

Where I come from, bell hooks's insistence that love is an ethic, not a feeling, never sounded sentimental to me. It named something I had been trying to build without words. She calls us toward a love ethic, toward an understanding of love as practice, woven from care, commitment, responsibility, respect, trust, and knowledge. Taking that seriously means accepting responsibility for how we show up with one another and letting love be measured by what we do rather than by what we claim to feel. In that light, accountability does not stand in opposition to love. It becomes one of love's clearest tests, the moment when love stops being abstract and enters the specific, sometimes excruciating detail of harm, power, and consequence.

For those of us raised on punishment, accountability will at first feel like danger, not care. Many of us have learnt to hear "we need to talk" as the start of rejection, humiliation, or control rather than a chance to deepen the relationship. We pull back in whatever way we can. That pull is our bodies remembering the times when being wrong meant being unlovable.

The punitive way of doing accountability fails because it turns behaviour into identity. One harmful act is taken as proof that a person is rotten at the core. They are treated as if the worst thing they have done is the whole story of who they are, and the conversation moves away from repair and toward getting rid of them. This pattern is not only personal.

Institutions often answer harm with punishment instead of looking for repair. In close relationships, people cut each other off rather than staying in contact long enough to work through what happened. In political life, harm is turned into a public drama that creates shame and headlines while very little actually changes. Online, the same habits are amplified. People are cast out with a few posts or comments, and the speed of reaction leaves almost no space for nuance, reflection, or repair. Even the way many adults speak to children passes on the same lesson: that making a mistake means you are the problem.

Children learn very early whether a mistake means "I did something wrong" or "there is something wrong with me." That difference characterises them. Studies of parent-child relationships suggest that shame and guilt do not work in the same ways. Harsh, punitive environments are linked to stronger and more damaging shame responses, while warmer and more supportive relationships make it easier for children to recognise wrongdoing without collapsing into worthlessness. Shame goes after the self. Guilt, when it is taken seriously in a caring relationship, can turn a child's attention toward impact and repair. Where shame freezes, hides, and attacks back, guilt held in a trusting relationship can open a doorway to empathy and connection. A child who is repeatedly addressed as if they are the problem does not learn to do better. They learn to be vigilant, avoidant, or highly skilled at defence. Raising children with integrity means holding a fierce line on behaviour while protecting, just as fiercely, their sense of being worthy of love and belonging. As hooks insists, to love children is to acknowledge their rights and their dignity, not to treat them as property to be controlled. Without justice, there can be no love.

Many adults carry that unprotected younger self into every difficult conversation. The face across the table may be fully grown, but the reaction comes from much earlier, from the part of a person that learnt love could be withdrawn at the first sign of failure. In that sense, much of the resistance to accountability is not resistance to truth but to humiliation, exile, or emotional collapse. The act of denying responsibility becomes a means of maintaining one's integrity when accountability has always been seen as an attack.

None of this should be seen as a justification for engaging in harmful behaviours. Still, the damage is significant. However, taking harm seriously also means asking how defensiveness was learnt and how it protects people from feeling the full weight of what they have done. Work in restorative justice is helpful here because it shifts the question. Instead of asking only who is guilty and how they should be punished, the focus turns to what happened, who was affected, and what repair is needed. In many circles, that practice is literal. People sit together in the same room and speak about impact, responsibility, and what needs to change so that the same harm does not repeat. The point is not to erase what happened, but to face it together and decide what repair is possible. This sort of accountability is not more lenient; rather, it simply is more demanding of each and every person in the room.

When harm is met with punishment, people often learn to act sorry without being genuinely touched. They grow quiet, say what they think is expected, play the part of remorse, and wait for the storm to pass. Nothing in the relationship has to change. Loving accountability asks for something harder. It stays with what happened and with its impact until both people can feel the weight of it. In that space, the person is always more than the harm, and the harm is acknowledged because the person matters. These truths are held together. You try to keep faith with the person while still saying, as clearly as you can, that what they did cannot continue.

The actual work starts now, developing through conversation and focused attention. The focus shifts toward understanding the impact of what has happened, while holding responsibility in a way that does not collapse into self-hatred or punishment. Change takes shape gradually, as patterns of interaction are reworked and something more intentional begins to guide the way you meet each other. In the end, practising this kind of accountability may sound simple, holding at once that you matter and that this behaviour cannot continue. What sustains it is a steadiness that neither hardens into judgement nor slips into neglect. Care becomes deliberate, shaped by boundaries that make continued connection possible. From within that clarity, the future is no longer assumed but slowly reimagined, carried by a language that can hold both truth and attachment at once.

Instead of reading someone's character, attention returns to what actually happened. The relationship becomes the starting point, making it possible to speak from care for what exists between you and a belief that something more honest can take shape. The tone changes as accusations like "you always" or "you are" begin to fall away. What replaces them is a more grounded way of speaking, one that stays close to experience, names impact, and makes room for change. Softer language does not dilute the truth; it removes some of the theatre of shame and opens space for accountability that can be lived rather than performed.

To hold each other in this way asks for vulnerability from the one who raises the issue. Calling someone in with love means acknowledging that it matters enough for silence to feel like abandonment. The discomfort of honesty is chosen over the false peace of keeping everything inside. There is care in that choice, and there is risk. Love, in this sense, is not simple reassurance but a willingness to enter conflict without letting it become a reason to end the relationship.

The person who is called in also has work to do. In a punitive culture, the first impulse is often to protect your sense of self at any cost, and that protection can quickly become another way of avoiding responsibility. A different kind of listening is needed. Accountability can be heard not as a verdict on who you are, but as information about what you did and what it did to someone else. Sometimes it is as simple, and as difficult, as reminding yourself that the words are about your actions, not your worth, so that you can keep listening instead of bracing for a final sentence.

This shift rarely happens all at once. It takes time and practice. Without it, conversations about harm can begin to resemble a courtroom, where the only available outcomes are conviction or acquittal. Slower, more deliberate responses make something else possible. You might notice your defensiveness while still recognising the other person's perspective, or acknowledge that harm was caused regardless of intent. At times, it means asking for space to think so that you can respond rather than react. These are not ways of proving innocence, but of staying in the conversation long enough for something other than reflex to emerge, allowing guilt to move toward reflection instead of collapsing into shame.

When it comes to accountability, a love-based practice has to work on both sides. The one who names harm is asked to resist the pull of punishment, including the lure of superiority and the desire to win. This requires stepping away from simple stories with clear villains and victims, and from the satisfaction of feeling justified within them. The task is to remain with what actually happened, without turning the other person into a symbol.

The one who is called in faces a different demand. It involves turning away from defensiveness, denial, and the impulse to retreat into self-victimisation, as well as letting go of the idea that good intentions cancel out harm. Staying present long enough to hear the impact can feel almost unbearable, yet it is part of the work. Both people are being asked to step outside the familiar logic of guilt and to trust, at least in part, that discomfort can move something rather than only wound it. This is difficult because punishment often presents itself as seriousness; what looks like moral clarity can feel like the only responsible response, especially when harm is still raw. Yet punishment abandons the slower labour of transformation, trading depth for speed and change for the relief of a quick conclusion. A love-based accountability asks for something more sustained, a continued willingness to examine oneself, to speak more carefully, and to choose differently over time. It holds on to the possibility of a future without denying the weight of the past, and asks people to remain in the work of repair even when it would be easier to withdraw or harden into certainty.

In many relationships, people only discover what they expect from each other once conflict has already broken open. A love-based practice tries to move some of that work earlier. Partners, friends, and family can talk in advance about how harm is brought up, how to pause without disappearing, what repair might look like, and which patterns they are no longer willing to repeat. This is less about scripting every argument and more about establishing, before anything erupts, that conflict is expected, that care does not depend on perfection, and that everyone involved intends to stay accountable when things become difficult.

The same ethic can shape family life. Children can learn that they remain lovable even when they have caused harm. At the same time, they come to understand that actions have consequences, and that repair is part of living with others rather than an occasional performance after a crisis. Being held to account then becomes one of the ordinary ways love appears, not a sign that love is being withdrawn.

On a larger scale, institutions face their own version of this challenge. Many swing between bureaucratic punishment and vague therapeutic language, either turning harm into a file or dissolving it into abstraction. A more consistent form of rigour would mean building cultures where feedback, consequence, and growth can exist together in public and predictable ways, so that people know what happens when harm is named. In such places, harm is neither treated as an individual flaw to be expelled nor as something to be smoothed over, but taken up as a shared responsibility that calls for attention, learning, and, as far as possible, repair.

No set of practices removes the pain. Even at its most careful and loving, accountability still grazes the ego and touches places we would rather leave in the dark. To be called in, or to call someone else in, means agreeing to meet ourselves where we would rather not look. That encounter hurts. The point is not to deny that hurt or dress it up as growth, but to separate it from punishment. Pain is not automatically a sign that we are being cast out or condemned. Sometimes it is the feeling of a relationship insisting on truth, refusing the comfort of denial or pretending. Sometimes it is the ache of knowing that something more honest is being asked of us, that a different way of being together has become nonnegotiable.

The hardest work does not end with our own reactions. It is also cultural. Many of us have been trained to believe that being confronted means we are no longer loved, and that confronting someone else means becoming cruel. Those lessons run deep. They keep people away from the conversations that might allow relationships to change instead of quietly decay. Yet both stories are false.

A more demanding truth is that accountability can be one of the most tender forms of regard. To hold someone to account with love is to say, in words or in actions, that this mattered, that you still matter, that what happened cannot be swept aside, and that there is still a path, if you are willing to walk it, toward something better than denial or disappearance. That path is rarely smooth. It asks for courage, patience, and a tolerance for ambiguity. The difficulty is not a sign that love has failed. It is often the clearest sign that love is being taken seriously.

---

*This essay is also published on [Substack](https://substack.com/@daniaawin/p-197246747).*

A love-based accountability asks for something more sustained, a continued willingness to examine oneself, to speak more carefully, and to choose differently over time. It holds on to the possibility of a future without denying the weight of the past, and asks people to remain in the work of repair even when it would be easier to withdraw or harden into certainty.

In many relationships, people only discover what they expect from each other once conflict has already broken open. A love-based practice tries to move some of that work earlier. Partners, friends, and family can talk in advance about how harm is brought up, how to pause without disappearing, what repair might look like, and which patterns they are no longer willing to repeat. This is less about scripting every argument and more about establishing, before anything erupts, that conflict is expected, that care does not depend on perfection, and that everyone involved intends to stay accountable when things become difficult.

The same ethic can shape family life. Children can learn that they remain lovable even when they have caused harm. At the same time, they come to understand that actions have consequences, and that repair is part of living with others rather than an occasional performance after a crisis. Being held to account then becomes one of the ordinary ways love appears, not a sign that love is being withdrawn.

On a larger scale, institutions face their own version of this challenge. Many swing between bureaucratic punishment and vague therapeutic language, either turning harm into a file or dissolving it into abstraction. A more consistent form of rigour would mean building cultures where feedback, consequence, and growth can exist together in public and predictable ways, so that people know what happens when harm is named. In such places, harm is neither treated as an individual flaw to be expelled nor as something to be smoothed over, but taken up as a shared responsibility that calls for attention, learning, and, as far as possible, repair.

No set of practices removes the pain. Even at its most careful and loving, accountability still grazes the ego and touches places we would rather leave in the dark. To be called in, or to call someone else in, means agreeing to meet ourselves where we would rather not look. That encounter hurts. The point is not to deny that hurt or dress it up as growth, but to separate it from punishment. Pain is not automatically a sign that we are being cast out or condemned. Sometimes it is the feeling of a relationship insisting on truth, refusing the comfort of denial or pretending. Sometimes it is the ache of knowing that something more honest is being asked of us, that a different way of being together has become nonnegotiable.

The hardest work does not end with our own reactions. It is also cultural. Many of us have been trained to believe that being confronted means we are no longer loved, and that confronting someone else means becoming cruel. Those lessons run deep. They keep people away from the conversations that might allow relationships to change instead of quietly decay. Yet both stories are false.

A more demanding truth is that accountability can be one of the most tender forms of regard. To hold someone to account with love is to say, in words or in actions, that this mattered, that you still matter, that what happened cannot be swept aside, and that there is still a path, if you are willing to walk it, toward something better than denial or disappearance. That path is rarely smooth. It asks for courage, patience, and a tolerance for ambiguity. The difficulty is not a sign that love has failed. It is often the clearest sign that love is being taken seriously.
